name: modules
context: Terraform modules -- reusable, composable infrastructure packages for organizing and sharing HCL configurations
info: Terraform modules are the primary mechanism for packaging and reusing infrastructure code. A module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Every Terraform configuration has at least one module (the root module), and modules can call other modules to include their resources. Modules can be sourced from local paths, the Terraform Registry, GitHub, S3, GCS, or HTTP URLs. This branch covers module fundamentals, authoring best practices via [[terraform/modules/authoring]], the public and private registries via [[terraform/modules/registry]], and composing modules into complex infrastructure via [[terraform/modules/composition]].
triggers: use a module from the registry
body:
# Terraform Modules

A Terraform module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Modules are the main way to package and reuse resource configurations in Terraform. Every Terraform configuration has at least one module -- the **root module** -- which consists of the `.tf` files in the main working directory.

## What is a Module?

A module is simply a directory containing `.tf` files. When you run `terraform apply` in a directory, that directory is the root module. You can call other modules (child modules) to organize your infrastructure into logical, reusable components.

```hcl
# Root module calling a child module
module "vpc" {
  source  = "./modules/vpc"
  version = "1.0.0"

  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  environment = "production"
}
```

## Root Module vs Child Modules

| Aspect | Root Module | Child Module |
|--------|------------|---------------|
| Location | Working directory where `terraform apply` runs | Referenced via `source` argument |
| State | Owns the state file | Resources appear in caller's state |
| Variables | Set via `.tfvars`, env vars, or CLI flags | Set via module block arguments |
| Outputs | Printed after apply | Accessed via `module.<NAME>.<OUTPUT>` |
| Provider config | Defined here | Inherited from root (or passed explicitly) |

## Module Sources

Terraform supports multiple source types for locating modules:

```hcl
# Local path
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
}

# Terraform Registry
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"
}

# GitHub
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/terraform-aws-modules/terraform-aws-vpc?ref=v5.0.0"
}

# S3 bucket
module "vpc" {
  source = "s3::https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/my-bucket/modules/vpc.zip"
}

# GCS bucket
module "vpc" {
  source = "gcs::https://www.googleapis.com/storage/v1/my-bucket/modules/vpc.zip"
}

# Generic HTTP
module "vpc" {
  source = "https://example.com/modules/vpc.zip"
}
```

## Passing Variables and Accessing Outputs

Modules communicate through input variables and output values. The caller passes arguments to the module block; the module exposes results via outputs:

```hcl
# In the calling module
module "database" {
  source = "./modules/rds"

  # Input variables
  instance_class = "db.t3.medium"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15.4"
  vpc_id         = module.vpc.vpc_id          # Output from another module
  subnet_ids     = module.vpc.database_subnets # Output from another module
}

# Accessing module outputs
resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_endpoint" {
  name  = "/app/db-endpoint"
  type  = "String"
  value = module.database.endpoint
}
```

## Module Versioning

When using registry modules, pin versions with constraints to avoid breaking changes:

```hcl
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"   # >= 5.0.0, < 6.0.0 (pessimistic constraint)
}
```

Version constraint operators: `=` (exact), `!=` (exclude), `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`, `~>` (pessimistic). The `~>` operator is the most common -- it allows patch and minor updates while preventing major version bumps. See [[terraform/modules/registry#version-constraints]] for full details.

## When to Use Modules

- **DRY principle** -- avoid duplicating resource blocks across environments
- **Team boundaries** -- separate concerns (networking team owns VPC module, app team consumes it)
- **Standardization** -- enforce organizational patterns (tagging, naming, security groups)
- **Testing** -- test infrastructure components in isolation
- **Sharing** -- publish to a registry for cross-team or community use

sections.title: Module fundamentals
sections.body: A module is a directory of `.tf` files. The root module is the working directory. Child modules are called via `module` blocks with a `source` argument. After adding or changing module sources, run `terraform init` to download modules. Modules appear in `.terraform/modules/` locally. Each module call creates a separate namespace -- resources in different module calls do not conflict.

sections.title: Module sources
sections.body: Local paths (`./modules/vpc`) are resolved relative to the calling module. Registry sources (`terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws`) use the format `<NAMESPACE>/<NAME>/<PROVIDER>`. GitHub sources support `?ref=` for branch, tag, or commit pinning. S3 and GCS sources reference zipped module archives. HTTP sources download `.zip` or `.tar.gz` archives. Private git repos use SSH URLs (`git::ssh://git@github.com/org/repo.git`). See [[terraform/modules/registry]] for registry-specific details.

sections.title: Variables and outputs
sections.body: Modules accept input via `variable` blocks and expose results via `output` blocks. Callers set inputs as arguments in the `module` block and read outputs with `module.<NAME>.<OUTPUT>`. Required variables have no `default`. Sensitive variables and outputs are redacted from CLI output. Use `description` on every variable and output for documentation. Variable validation blocks enforce constraints at plan time. See [[terraform/modules/authoring#best-practices]] for design guidance.

sections.title: Module versioning
sections.body: Registry modules support semantic versioning with constraint operators: `=` (exact), `!=` (not equal), `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`, `~>` (pessimistic). The `~>` operator is recommended -- `~> 5.0` allows `5.x` but not `6.0`. Local modules do not support version constraints. For git-sourced modules, pin with `?ref=v1.2.3` tags. Always pin versions in production to prevent unexpected changes. See [[terraform/modules/registry#version-constraints]].
